title: Ten years of Descemet membrane endothelial keratoplasty: Identifying risk factors and early failure signs.
abstract: To evaluate the effect of surgical indications and complexity on long-term clinical outcomes in Descemet membrane endothelial keratoplasty (DMEK) and identify early signs of graft failure. Retrospective case series of 105 patients who underwent DMEK from March 2012 to December 2014. Surgical results were analyzed at 1 year, 3 years, 5 years, and 10 years based on surgical indication: fuchs endothelial dystrophy (FECD) (n = 47) and bullous keratopathy (BK) (n = 58), and on the presence of anterior segment comorbidities: simple (n = 63) versus complex (n = 42) DMEK. Fifty-two patients (54 eyes) were followed up for 3 years, 35 patients (42 eyes) reached the 5-year follow-up, and 20 patients (26 eyes) achieved the 10-year follow-up. Simple DMEK procedures and FECD demonstrated significantly better best corrected visual acuity (BCVA) compared to complex DMEK and BK at 1 year, 3 years, 5 years, and 10 years, respectively (P < 0.01). No statistically significant difference in endothelial cell density (ECD) decline was noted between simple and complex DMEK procedures, nor between FECD and BK (P > 0.05). Central and peripheral pachymetry were significantly higher in BK and complex surgeries at 10 years. While DMEK represents a promising therapeutic avenue for corneal decompensation alongside anterior segment comorbidities, extended follow-up indicates a rise in central and peripheral pachymetry in comparison with simple DMEK. This increase could serve as an early indicators of corneal decompensation, potentially leading to reduced survival rates.
full_text:
Ten years of Descemet membrane endothelial keratoplasty: Identifying risk factors and early failure signs

Purpose:

To evaluate the effect of surgical indications and complexity on long-term clinical outcomes in Descemet membrane endothelial keratoplasty (DMEK) and identify early signs of graft failure.

Design:

Retrospective case series of 105 patients who underwent DMEK from March 2012 to December 2014.

Methods:

Surgical results were analyzed at 1 year, 3 years, 5 years, and 10 years based on surgical indication: fuchs endothelial dystrophy (FECD) (n = 47) and bullous keratopathy (BK) (n = 58), and on the presence of anterior segment comorbidities: simple (n = 63) versus complex (n = 42) DMEK.

Results:

Fifty-two patients (54 eyes) were followed up for 3 years, 35 patients (42 eyes) reached the 5-year follow-up, and 20 patients (26 eyes) achieved the 10-year follow-up. Simple DMEK procedures and FECD demonstrated significantly better best corrected visual acuity (BCVA) compared to complex DMEK and BK at 1 year, 3 years, 5 years, and 10 years, respectively (P < 0.01). No statistically significant difference in endothelial cell density (ECD) decline was noted between simple and complex DMEK procedures, nor between FECD and BK (P > 0.05). Central and peripheral pachymetry were significantly higher in BK and complex surgeries at 10 years.

Conclusion:

While DMEK represents a promising therapeutic avenue for corneal decompensation alongside anterior segment comorbidities, extended follow-up indicates a rise in central and peripheral pachymetry in comparison with simple DMEK. This increase could serve as an early indicators of corneal decompensation, potentially leading to reduced survival rates.

Since the first descemet membrane endothelial keratoplasty (DMEK) in 2006, this surgery has proven its superiority in treating endothelial disorders. Over the last decade, DMEK has become the first-line treatment for endothelial decompensation. Minimal surgically induced astigmatism, excellent visual acuity, and lower risk of graft rejection have popularized DMEK over descemet stripping (automated) endothelial keratoplasty (DSAEK) and penetrating keratoplasty (PKP). Higher 5-year graft survival and better secondary outcomes after DMEK have been observed. While short- and long-term results of DMEK for fuchs endothelial dystrophy (FECD) have been reported with excellent outcomes, there is limited data on the long-term results of DMEK in bullous keratopathy (BK). Vasiliauskaitė et al. found a 79% graft survival rate at 10 years, a 68% decrease in endothelial cell density (ECD), and stable best corrected visual acuity (BCVA) in 89% of patients, with their cohort comprising 94 FECD eyes and only six BK eyes. Similarly, long-term results of DMEK were reported by Bichet et al. with only 3 BK eyes. Only two studies have reported on DMEK in BK, finding higher recurrence of central and peripheral edema and a lower survival rate in the BK group. In this study, we aimed to report up to 10-year outcomes of DMEK in FECD and BK and to evaluate the effect of surgery complexity on long-term clinical outcomes.

Methods

Study setting

All patients who underwent DMEK by a single surgeon (AS) from March 2012 to December 2014 were included [Table 1]. Patients who did not achieve the first-year follow-up (30/87 patients) were excluded from the analysis. The donor corneoscleral tissues were obtained from the French Eye Bank (endothelial cell morphology and viability were assessed, and the grafts were stored free-floating in an organ culture medium). The research was approved by the hospital review board – IRB 0001280. The study adhered to the tenets of the Declaration of Helsinki, and informed consent was obtained from all patients before surgery.

Demographics of DMEK eyes and donors no statistically significant differences were found between the demographics of all operated patients and those included in the statistical analysis

Surgery

Donor corneas were prepared for DMEK on the day of surgery by the surgeon using the submerged cornea using the backgrounds away (SCUBA) peeling technique. The DMEK roll was stained with trypan blue 0.06% (VisionBlue; DORC International, USA). A “no-touch” technique was used for graft insertion and unrolling: three side ports and a 3.0-mm clear cornea tunnel incision were made. Complete air fill of the anterior chamber was performed, then descemet’s membrane (DM) was scored and peeled using an inverted Sinskey hook and a 90° spatula (Moria SA, Antony, France). The graft was injected into the anterior chamber (AC) using a DMEK implantation cartridge (Geuder AG, Heidelberg, Baden-Württemberg, Germany). Careful, indirect manipulation of the tissue was performed to unfold the graft in the correct orientation, then an air bubble was injected underneath the donor DM. The AC was filled with air for 45 minutes then pressurized by an air–liquid exchange. Triple DMEK, defined as DMEK combined with cataract surgery, was performed whenever cataracts coexisted with endothelial dysfunction. A single-piece monofocal hydrophobic acrylic intraocular lens was implanted in these cases.

Postoperative follow-up and medication

Post-operatively, patients were evaluated at 1 day, 1 week, and 1, 3, and 6 months, then yearly after the surgery. At each visit, the following parameters were recorded: BCVA, central corneal thickness (CCT), peripheral corneal thickness at the 5mm optical zone superiorly (sPCT5), inferiorly (iPCT5), nasally (nPCT5), and temporally (tPCT5), peripheral corneal thickness at the 7 mm optical zone superiorly (sPCT7), inferiorly (iPCT7), nasally (nPCT7), and temporally (tPCT7), as well as ECD. Additionally, intraoperative and postoperative complications were documented. CCT, peripheral corneal thickness (PCT), and graft detachment were evaluated using OCT Visante® (Carl Zeiss AG, Oberkochen, Baden-Württemberg, Germany). Graft detachment was defined as minor (<1/3 of the graft surface area) or major detachment (>1/3 of the graft surface area). A repeat air injection (rebubbling) into the AC was performed in case of clinically significant graft detachment during the early postoperative period. ECD was measured using a specular microscope CEM-530 multi-area specular microscopy (NIDEK, Gamagori, Japan). Automatic cell border alignments and measurements were evaluated by an independent examiner and corrected manually in case of misalignment. Postoperative medication included topical ciprofloxacin 0.3% and tobramycin 0.3% 4 times daily for 1 week, topical dexamethasone 0.1% 4 times daily for 4 weeks, tapered to once daily over a period of 6 months, followed by fluorometholone 0.1% 2 times daily for 3 months, then once daily indefinitely. Allograft rejection was defined as the presence of an endothelial rejection line (Khodadoust line) or inflammatory keratic precipitates (on slit lamp biomicroscopy) or an increase in corneal thickness >30µm. Survival time was defined as the time between surgery and the last available follow-up or graft failure. Primary graft failure (PMG) was defined as an absence of corneal clearance at 8 weeks post-operatively in an eye with full graft attachment. Secondary graft failure (SGF) was defined as corneal decompensation after an interval of corneal clearance.

Statistical analysis

Statistical analysis was performed with the SPSS statistical software package, version 24.0 (SPSS, Chicago, IL), and Excel software for Windows (Microsoft, Redmond, Washington). Student’s t-test was used to compare parametric variables, and the Wilcoxon rank-sum test was applied to non-parametric variables. Correlations were studied using the Pearson correlation method. Values are shown as mean ± standard deviation unless otherwise specified. All P values (P) were two-sided, and P values < 0.05 were considered significant. Kaplan-Meier analysis was performed to evaluate survival curves, and the equality of the survival functions was compared using a Wilcoxon test and the Tarone-Ware test.

Results

A total of 105 eyes from 87 patients were included in the study. We analyzed surgical results based on two classifications. First, we categorized DMEK procedures into two groups: FECD (n = 47) and BK (n = 58), for the purpose of comparison with previous studies. Second, we categorized surgeries as either simple or complex. “Simple DMEK” (n = 63) were surgeries in which endothelial dysfunction was isolated, while cases with anterior segment comorbidities (AC-IOL, extensive peripheral synechiae, previous PKP, ICE syndrome, presence of vitreous in the anterior chamber) were classified as “complex cases” (n = 42). There were no isolated cases of simple FECD in the complex DMEK group. Even when the contralateral eye had FECD, the eye was considered part of the complex surgery group if other anterior segment comorbidities were present. There were no significant differences in the donor eye characteristics between the individual groups, including FECD versus BK and simple versus complex cases.

After the first postoperative year, 35.5% of patients (30 patients/33 eyes) were lost to follow-up in our department and were followed up by their respective referring ophthalmologists. The mean follow-up duration was 5.12 ± 3.3 years (range: 1–11 years). Table 1 presents the demographics of patients who achieved the 1-year follow-up and were included in the outcome analysis. Fifty-two patients (54 eyes) were followed up for 3 years, 35 patients (42 eyes) reached the 5-year follow-up, and 20 patients (26 eyes) achieved the 10-year follow-up. Tables 2 and 3 show postoperative results depending on surgical complexity and indication, respectively.

Visual acuity

Simple DMEK procedures and FECD demonstrated statistically significantly better BCVA compared to complex DMEK cases and BK at 1 year, 3 years, 5 years, and 10 years, respectively (P < 0.01) [Table 2]. Fig. 1 shows visual acuity at different follow-up visits according to surgical indication and complexity.

Graph representation of BCVA progression over the follow-up period. BCVA lines are significantly different. BCVA increases in both groups during the first 6 months and then stabilizes over 10 years

Endothelial cell density

Overall, the average preoperative donor ECD was 2728 ± 153 cells/mm², which decreased to 1829 ± 293 cells/mm² at 1 month, 1613 ± 463 cells/mm² at 1 year, 1120 ± 485 cells/mm² at 3 years, 1075 ± 506 cells/mm² at 5 years, and 975 ± 506 cells/mm² at 10 years. This corresponded to an ECD decline of 33%, 41%, 59%, 61%, and 65% compared to preoperative ECD values at 1 month, 1 year, 3 years, 5 years, and 10 years, respectively. We found no statistically significant difference in ECD decline between simple and complex DMEK procedures, nor between FECD and BK (P > 0.05) [Tables 2 and 3, Fig. 2]. An ECD of <1,000 cells/mm² was found in 50% of the eyes at 3 years, 55% at 5 years, and 61% at 10 years, respectively.

Graph representation of ECD loss over the follow-up period

Pachymetry

The average preoperative CCT was 660 ± 88 µm, which decreased to 542 ± 75 µm at 1 month, 522 ± 42 µm at 1 year, 522 ± 45 µm at 3 years, 518 ± 49 µm at 5 years, and 516 ± 60 µm at 10 years. Fig. 3 shows CCT at different follow-up visits according to surgical indication and complexity. There was no statistically significant difference in central and peripheral pachymetry measured at 5 and 7 mm between FECD and BK at 1 year. Complex DMEK exhibited significantly higher nPCT5, sPCT5, and sPCT7 at 1 year post-operatively (P < 0.02). Beyond the first postoperative year, when DMEK was categorized by surgical complexity or indication, there were no significant differences in CCT and peripheral corneal thickness (PCT) between the groups (P > 0.05) at 3 and 5 years post-operatively. However, at 10 years post-operatively, both FECD and simple DMEK showed significantly lower central and peripheral pachymetry compared to BK and complex DMEK (P < 0.05), respectively. When comparing changes within each subgroup between 5 and 10 years, central pachymetry change showed no significant difference between the FECD and BK groups. However, four out of eight peripheral parameters were significantly different between FECD and BK, while two out of eight parameters were significantly different between the complex and simple surgery groups. Additionally, two other parameters demonstrated borderline significance, with P values of 0.051 Supplemental Files 1 and 2.

Postoperative complications

Rebubbling was required in 12.4% of eyes (n = 13). The mean time between DMEK surgery and the last rebubbling was 10 ± 7 days (median 7 days, range 1–35 days). These patients were followed up for 5.43 ± 1.81 years (range 1–10 years), and seven were available for the 10-year follow-up. Among patients who required rebubbling, two experienced graft failure: one case of primary failure, which was lost to follow-up, and one case of SGF at 3 years. Both patients had undergone complex surgeries, including post-PKP and BK secondary to AC-IOL.

Fourteen patients underwent DMEK for corneal decompensation resulting from AC-IOL. These patients were monitored for an average duration of 2.8 ± 2.6 years. Among them, eight reached the 1-year follow-up, five reached the 3-year follow-up, four reached the 5-year follow-up, and two reached the 10-year follow-up milestones. At the 3-year mark, patients who underwent combined IOL exchange with posterior chamber IOL implantation (scleral fixated IOL) (n = 5) demonstrated a mean BCVA of 0.55 ± 0.53 logMAR, a CCT of 539 ± 34 µm, and an ECD of 1751 ± 521 cells/mm². Patients who retained their AC-IOL (n = 7) exhibited a mean BCVA of 0.83 ± 0.53 logMAR, a CCT of 523 ± 34 µm, and an ECD of 1380 ± 503 cells/mm² at 3 years. One graft failure occurred at the 3-year mark in a patient who retained their AC-IOL, while another secondary failure emerged at 5 years in a patient who underwent combined IOL exchange and DMEK.

Graft survival

The overall mean survival time was 9.2 ± 0.26 years. The survival rate differed significantly between subgroups: FECD patients had a significantly higher survival rate than those with BK (P = 0.032), and simple DMEK had a significantly higher survival rate than complex DMEK (P = 0.008). Fig. 4 presents the Kaplan-Meier survival graph for the different subgroups. Twelve eyes experienced graft failure and were planned for repeated DMEK: four were from the simple DMEK group, and eight were from the complex cases category. Three cases required secondary DMEK due to primary graft failure and underwent reoperation within 5 months post-DMEK. All three (1 from the simple DMEK group and 2 from the complex DMEK group) had a successful early postoperative course, with visual improvement. SGFs (nine eyes) were more frequently noted in complex cases (six/25 eyes) than in simple DMEK (four/47 eyes) and in BK (eight/24 eyes) than in FECD (one/44 eyes). They occurred at a mean time of 8.11 ± 1.97 years post-operatively (median = 8 years): at 9.3 ± 0.58 years in the simple DMEK group and at 7.6 ± 2.42 years in the complex group.

Kaplan-Meier survival graph overall (top), comparing surgical complexity (middle), and comparing surgical indication (bottom). Up to 8 years post-operatively, complex DMEK exhibited the lowest survival rate. Note that the number of patients available for follow-up at 9–11 years was limited; therefore, conclusions regarding differential survival rates between subgroups may not be definitive

Discussion

This study aimed to evaluate the long-term outcomes of DMEK in various types of endothelial dysfunction. The overall 8-year graft survival probability was 82%, consistent with findings from previous studies. Another larger cohort reported a lower 5-year survival probability for the first 250 eyes compared to the next 250 eyes (88% versus 94%, respectively). This difference may be attributed to the learning curve associated with DMEK, which could impact graft survival even after performing over 100 procedures. Future studies may show higher 10-year survival rates, but our cohort is aligned with previous research, demonstrating that most eyes operated on during the pioneering phase of DMEK had excellent and stable clinical outcomes, with promising graft longevity over the first-decade post-surgery.

The probability of requiring repeat keratoplasty was 5% at 1 year and 26% at 10 years, consistent with previous findings. Baydoun et al. reported a five-fold increase in the hazard risk of graft failure in BK compared to FECD. This has been attributed to higher levels of inflammatory cytokines in comorbid eyes, increasing the risk of endothelial cell loss and graft failure. Our study found that complex cases were more likely to require repeated keratoplasty than simpler DMEK cases (32% vs. 11%). Additionally, BK cases had a higher likelihood of needing repeated DMEK compared to FECD cases (33% vs. 4.5%). It is noteworthy that in our cohort, preoperative pachymetry and visual acuity were worse in eyes with BK than in those with FECD, likely due to the presence of preoperative intraocular inflammation, which can further compromise postoperative outcomes in BK.

Consistent with previous publications, our cohort showed a linear decline in ECD between 6 months and 5 years post-operatively. Price et al. documented a significant decrease in the rate of ECD decline after 8 years post-operatively in DMEK. Our findings support this, showing that after 5 years, the rate of ECD loss slows down. Eyes with an ECD of less than 1,000 cells/mm² at the 10-year follow-up maintained stable ECD and BCVA. A low ECD does not necessarily result in corneal decompensation; even with an ECD below 500 cells/mm², a low cell count may still maintain clear corneal homeostasis and stable corneal thickness.

Progressive ECD loss does not significantly impact BCVA as long as corneal transparency, indicated by CCT, is maintained. In our cohort, when DMEK surgeries were classified into FECD and BK groups, CCT remained stable with no significant differences in central and peripheral pachymetry at 1, 3, and 5 years post-operatively. However, by the 10-year mark, there was a significant increase in CCT and PCT in the BK group. Concurrently, the BCVA curve exhibited a steeper decline in the BK group, indicating greater visual loss compared to the FECD group. When surgeries were categorized by complexity, complex DMEK showed thicker peripheral pachymetry at one year compared to simple DMEK. This increase in thickness resolved at 3 years but reappeared at 10 years. An increase in pachymetry is highly related to accelerated ECD loss and might indicate a potential risk of future graft failure. Over a longer follow-up period, the reoperation rate is expected to be higher in BK and complex surgeries with anterior segment comorbidities.

Zwingelberg et al. assessed the evolution of peripheral pachymetry over the postoperative period in BK and FECD patients. They reported a faster recurrence of peripheral and central corneal edema in eyes with BK compared to those with FECD. PCT increased from 12 months after DMEK in the BK group, and this increase correlated with accelerated ECD loss. Although their cohort included only 10% of BK patients and did not provide information on ocular comorbidities and surgical complexity, our results confirm their findings. To our knowledge, only one study has reported on DMEK where FECD was not the primary indication. Their findings align closely with ours, showing higher survival probabilities for eyes operated on for FECD compared to eyes operated on for BK. In our cohort, a significantly greater increase in peripheral pachymetry was observed in the complex and BK groups over 10 years. This likely reflects an earlier progression of peripheral pachymetry changes over time, suggesting a decline in endothelial cell function. These findings indicate that peripheral pachymetry could serve as an early marker of reduced endothelial function.

Complex DMEK surgery exhibited continuous improvement in BCVA up until 3 years post-operatively. In contrast, when surgeries were classified according to indication, the BK group showed a rapid increase in BCVA at 6 months, followed by stabilization at a plateau until 10 years. Complex cases often involve stromal scars or corneal neovascularization due to chronic severe endothelial dysfunction, and DMEK can reestablish endothelial function in these cases, leading to prolonged corneal healing, neovascularization regression, and sustained improvement in BCVA. Therefore, surgery should be considered for various cases, including comorbid eyes, but patient expectations need to be addressed accordingly. In our series, patients with AC-IOLs that were not exchanged did not experience adverse effects on visual improvement or graft survival compared to the rest of the cohort. We chose this approach to avoid excessive intraocular manipulation, which could potentially elevate the risk of postoperative complications such as macular edema or glaucoma. Although no definitive conclusions can be drawn due to the small sample size, our patients showed favorable long-term outcomes.

While our study provides valuable insights into the long-term outcomes of DMEK, it is essential to acknowledge its limitations. First, the retrospective nature of this study may introduce inherent biases and incomplete data documentation. Our loss to follow-up is high but comparable to previous studies. Patients who underwent uncomplicated surgery and experienced satisfactory early postoperative recovery were particularly lost to follow-up as they continued care with their referring physicians. Second, the study was conducted at a single center, which may limit the generalizability of our findings. Additionally, the early phase of the DMEK technique during our study period may have influenced outcomes, with subsequent advancements in surgical techniques possibly yielding different results. Third, the absence of IOP data collection beyond the first postoperative years may represent another limitation of this study. Although it was not the primary focus, elevated IOP may play a role in SGF and is a factor to consider. This study did not specifically investigate or categorize the causes of SGF, including the potential interaction between IOP and other comorbidities that could influence both graft failure and glaucoma risk.

Conclusion

In conclusion, DMEK procedures involving complex cases can yield favorable long-term outcomes, though potentially not as favorable as those in simpler DMEK procedures. BK cases are inherently more complex than FECD and are associated with lower long-term graft survival. An increase in peripheral pachymetry may be an early sign of future graft failure and should be closely monitored.
